chunks.text: from MFE quickly introduce themselves, because they're here to answer anything particularly technical. Yeah. Good morning, Sarah Kenwood. I'm the manager, the HSNO policy manager at MFE. And morning, I'm Julie Everett-Hates. I'm a principal advisor to this program. Yeah. Thank you very much. Appreciate it.

It's an early start for everyone today. But look, I just would like to acknowledge from the outset that I did come into this portfolio quite late once the policy work had already largely been done. So my role now really is to shepherd the bill through the parliamentary process, which I'm looking to you as my parliamentary colleagues for your help and assistance with that. But I'm really keen to ensure that the draft as it is delivers on its intended purpose, and that is very much how we improve the operation of the system in practice.

So that's how I'm viewing it, as a system improvement bill. The HSNO framework is and always will be fundamentally about protecting people and communities and the environment. But I think we have all heard, I know we all engage in rural communities, we've all heard that aspects of that system have become really complex, really cumbersome, burdensome, and don't always achieve the purpose that user groups are looking for.

So it's very much about addressing those issues, but in a measured and practical way. So what we're trying to achieve at this point in time is by removing those unnecessary barriers, improving the clarity for user groups, reducing the duplication, which I'm sure you've all heard time and time again, and making that process as efficient as we can, but within the really important and very stringent parameters of the environmental protections that very much sit at the heart of this legislation.

So we're very much aiming for system operations improvement. I could go on, but in practical effect is that the changes should mean, hopefully will mean, that for farmers and growers particularly, they can access new chemicals, biological controls, particularly for pest protection, crop protection, and also that the bill, as you've seen in the draft, it's aiming to enable better use of the assessments from trusted overseas regulators, in order, as I keep saying, to reduce that duplication, to streamline the system, and then,

of course, to apply our New Zealand specific safeguards. But I don't want to talk at you, colleagues, so please, by all means, feel free to ask some questions of Sarah, Julie, or myself. Steve. Yeah. Thank you for being here, Minister. Yes. Is it your intent that the bill allow greater leeway for determinations to be made around the definition of new organisms?

Yeah, I was expecting that question to come up, and I've seen a number of submitters have raised their concerns about the gene technology and GMOs and the potential overlap with that particular amendment bill. These are very separate, very separate issues.

You'll note that the gene technology amendment bill has been set on pause. So this specifically does not change the regulatory settings for genetically modified organisms, and it does not expand what is currently allowed. It doesn't alter that fundamental approach to regulating the organisms. So if that clarifies, but equally, if you'd like to ask the technical experts a bit more on that.

I'll ask a follow-up that might simplify it. Okay. So if it was our finding that it did simplify that, then you would be happy for us to propose amendments to rein it back in so that it doesn't do more than what you just said? I'd prefer to cross that bridge if and when we get to it. But equally, would you like to perhaps comment on the possibility that finding a new way through?

Yeah, always open to finding new ways through. Yeah. Yeah. And final one. Yes, Steve. Likewise, it's not your intention that the bill allow a more rapid pathway from contained use of GMOs to environmental release of GMOs? No. No. Sorry, Minister.

Please. Yeah. So the same criteria, minimum standards, and other adverse effects will be still assessed and considered to make that assessment, whether it should be released. Okay. Yeah. Thank you. Mark. Morning, Minister. Sorry, I'm online.

Lovely to see you as always, Minister Greig. You talk about duplication, and suffice it to say duplication adds inherent costs. Do you want to sort of enlighten those that are online what duplication we currently are facing and how we might save money and create economic activity if we remove said duplication?

You want specific examples? Well, not necessarily specific, but give some guidance to the listeners online that what duplication we're currently facing and how we might alleviate that. I might turn to Sarah for that question, Mark. Thank you. Yeah. Thanks, Sarah. It's quite operational. No, quite. So I think specifically what we're talking about with duplication is that the current model requires a sort of one size fits all approach to applications coming in. What we're proposing is a more risk-based and proportionate

approach, which means that for lower risk substances, you wouldn't have to provide all of the information that you would, for example, for a new Category C application. It's being risk-based and proportionate, reducing and streamlining some of the application pathways and entry points. Not relaxing the risk assessment of those or the precautionary approach that the EPA takes in its consideration, but simply smoothing the process to allow things to move more quickly.

If that answers your question It absolutely does. Thank you all, team. I'm very grateful and lovely to see you. Any further questions? No, no, I'm fine, thank you, Mr. Chair. No, it doesn't seem any further questions. Is there any final comments you'd like to make? No, I was really just going to thank you for your consideration of this. Look, we're open to improving the bill where we possibly can, and obviously the technical experts are going to be here to assist you along the way. But, thank you very much for taking your time

to consider this. I gather there's a lot of submissions for you to get through, so, I'll look forward to your report back. Thank you. And thank you, Minister. I'd just like to acknowledge former Speaker of the House and Minister for Primary Industries, David Carter, who's in the audience as well. Oh, David. Welcome, David.
Negligent not doing that myself. All right, have a good morning. Thank you. Thank you.
Right. We'll kick on with hearing your evidence. Now we've got, first up is Horticulture New Zealand. If you'd like to come forward, please.

Morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. We've allocated 15 minutes for your presentation and questions, so the floor is in your hands. Way to go. Thank you. So, yeah, thank you for the opportunity to present. I'm Rebecca Fisher. I'm from Horticulture New Zealand.

With me is Chung Wa Shen, and from our risk policy team, and we're joined by Simon Cook, a kiwifruit grower, an avocado grower from the Bay of Plenty, and also a registered chemical applicator. Simon will provide practical examples from a grower's perspective. We will briefly highlight two key points from our submission and then welcome questions, so we'll definitely stick to time.

As you all know, HortNZ represents over 4,300 fruit and vegetable growers who contribute over 7.5 billion to our economy. The sector is enabling to double farm gate value by 2035, but this depends on growers having access to efficacious crop protection products. HortNZ supports the government's objectives of improving growers'

access to crop protection products, and as such, we do support the direction of reform. Our recommendations are intended to help ensure the bill delivers the outcomes it was designed to achieve, while maintaining robust environmental practices and protections. So I'd like to focus on three areas today.

The first, recognizing products approved by trusted regulators overseas, as the previous speaker sort of covered. New Zealand is a country of 5 million people. We cannot realistically replicate the scientific capability of much larger countries, nor can we expect companies to undertake bespoke, extensive regulatory processes for such a small market as ours.

Recognizing products approved by trusted overseas regulators is not simply an efficiency measure, it is actually a practical necessity. If a crop protection product is registered in a trusted country, the data provided to the overseas regulator should satisfy requirements for it to be registered in New Zealand without reanalyzing the overseas data. This would eliminate duplication, as was said on the screen there, of what our trusted countries have already done. Any additional assessment to manage New Zealand risks should

be limited to genuine, specific New Zealand issues. Global harmonization of crop protection products should be the default approach by ACVM and EPA, as is done with human medicines, where Medsafe leverages international assessments. And as such, in our submission, we recommend establishing a standalone international pathway for this that is mandatory with limited exclusions.

So our second point, application of the precautionary approach. HortNZ supports the precautionary approach and strong environmental protections. Our concern is not with protection itself, but with ensuring it is applied proportionately and consistently. Scientific uncertainty is inherent in all risk assessments, and regulatory decisions are rarely made with complete certainty.

But it is important to distinguish between hazard and actual practical risk, and between theoretical exposure pathways and likely exposure under real-world conditions. It is also important to consider benefits when assessing risks. We are not seeking lower standards, rather greater clarity and consistency around how precaution is applied.

We recommend a policy framework is created that outlines the best course of action if there is uncertainty, and sets out how precaution is applied in practice, including to give effect to the full purposes of the act, specifically economic, social, and cultural well-being. Currently, EPA implements a very strict precautionary approach, which influences decision-making processes, and we are asking for an approach that is proportionate to risk.

A third and final point we'd like to emphasize is that HortNZ does not support introducing a levy. Throughout our submission, we identify opportunities to improve efficiency using global data, reduced duplication of assessments and reassessments. This all means better use of regulatory resources. Utilizing the assessment done by overseas regulators will reduce duplication and significantly reduce the workload and resource required.

This opportunity should be implemented first. A new levy should be a measure of last resort rather than the starting point, after all avenues for efficiencies have been explored. A full list of our recommended amendments are in our submission, and overall, we do not see these as radical changes. Rather, they are practical changes intended to help ensure New Zealand growers have timely access to the same modern tools available as their international competitors, while maintaining robust science-based environmental protections.

We'd like to encourage the committee to use this opportunity to ensure the bill delivers meaningful benefits to growers to enable double farm gate value, for our vision for our sector to be realized. Thank you. Thanks. We'll go to Steve for the first question. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you very much for your substantive submission.

I gleaned that your number one concern is about greater ease of access to biopesticides. Can you quickly outline for us why that's important for you? Sure. Simon, would you like to? Not just biopesticides, but a whole range of chemicals. But in particular, biopesticides tend to be safer for the environment because they are a more natural source.

So that's, yeah, one of the key reasons we are interested in that source. You also, on page 15, say that you'd like to see a single joint regulator. But you comment that any such regulation should require ministerial approval to amend, not agency discretion alone. It should be accompanied by a mandatory public reporting requirement on compliance. I wonder if you can expand on what you're getting at there.

Sure. So I guess, yeah, that was the desire in our submissions. We see that the bills are being separate. So, we are okay with that. I guess it's the certainty and the regulations for the timelines, so that as a product goes through, when it's submitted to both regulators, there can be transparency so that chemical, companies know, crop protection companies know, but also our growers know when products may be released to market. So there's that transparency and timeline.

So that could be dealt with in the regulation. Final one. Regarding genetically modified organisms, are you anxious that there needs to be greater ease of access to those or faster pathways to environmental use of those? So we're awaiting further announcements from the government on the direction of that path of the bill and don't have further comment on that.

But that's not a high priority for you in terms of the HSNO Act? We probably don't have further comment on that today because we didn't cover it in our submission, so we- Yeah, so it's not one I-- Like, if there was concern that there was inadvertent greater leeway given to pathways to environmental release of GMOs and the committee wanted to rein that in, that's not an issue that's particularly of concern to you.

We agreed with the MFA and the minister's position on that question before, that we're waiting for that bridge and we'll wait for that to come and have, see the direction from the government on that one. We felt this was quite separate issues for our- Okay... crop protection products. It's good to understand. Thank you. So you're just saying that because there's no pathway at the moment, you haven't included it because it's not been considered?

Correct. Yeah, we're waiting on that separate bill. So we didn't put that in our submission. I think the submission was good. Yeah. Yeah. Any further questions? Well, it's helpful to have your recommendations as they set them out- Yes... to understand it. So thank you. Look, as Dana said, very good submission and certainly the areas that you've highlighted that you've got concerns with, we'll take into consideration as well. So thank you very much.
There don't seem to be any further questions. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

All right. Scheduled next are Physicians and Scientists for Global Responsibility New Zealand via Zoom. Are they- Here. Oh, good morning, Jodi. Good morning. How are you? Good, thanks. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. Mm. We've set aside 10 minutes for your submission.

Thank you. My n- The time is in your hands. Away you go. Thank you, Chair, and members. My name's Jodi Brunning. I'm a member of the Physicians and Scientists for Global Responsibility New Zealand. It's a 25-year-old charitable trust. I have a BBus Agribusiness, Masters in Sociology. My thesis was science policy, funding, and outcomes.

Now, the government has acknowledged that the risk assessment system is outdated. Yet instead of updating risk assessment methods and models first, it is legislating to make approvals easier, faster, and less transparent. So in effect, members are being asked to approve powers when the rules that underpin public trust and confidence are not fit for purpose.

So the bill cannot be risk proportionate at this stage. The regulatory impact statement problem definition concerns limited access, lengthy, and uncertain approval pathways. The RIS problem was not concerned with ensuring that the purpose and principles of the HSNO Act would be upheld following any legislative amendment. The members are not being asked to decide whether a particular pathway is safe. Members are being asked to create pathways before the underlying risk assessment evaluation has been published, consulted on, and independently reviewed.

The HSNO Act amendment bill arose from the recommendations made by the Ministry for Regulation, Agricultural and Horticultural Products Regulatory Review, yet that consultation was flawed. Back in 2024, the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment commented that the regulatory review consultation itself was too narrow.

Officials then invited expert stakeholders into the HSNO Amendment Bill consultation who stood to financially benefit from deregulation. So this includes former AgCam, the Animal and Plant Health Association of New Zealand that are funded by the agrochemical industry, and the Crown Research Institutes that MBIE funds to create biotechnology. So the people consulted were largely organizations whose institutional mission is to obtain approvals, develop and commercialise biotechnology, or accelerate product access. Missing were doctors, public health

experts, occupational disease experts, environmental toxicologists, consumer representatives, the organic sector, and the communities bearing the risks. A 2017 Treasury document, "Government Expectations for Good Regulatory Practice," requires that affected and interested parties should be granted appropriate opportunities to comment.

So two flawed consultations to date have failed to do this. The May 2025 RIS fails to observe the Treasury Government Expectations document in other ways. Treasury requires that systematic impact and risk analysis is undertaken, and that genuine effort is made to identify, understand, and estimate the various categories of cost and benefit. So there's no cost-benefit analysis in the RIS, astonishing, and the only cost-benefit language concerns levy funding for the regulated industries. So the government has stated that the EPA risk

assessment framework is outdated. Recommendation 10 of the Hort and Ag review stated, "Update the EPA's outdated risk assessment models and consider how to keep them up to date for the future." But until risk assessment processes and models are revised, incorporating the PCE's advice on environmental monitoring, risks cannot be understood and costs cannot be understood. So the process is upside down. The bill is premature. Fix risk assessment first, then legislate. So the Amendment Bill contains mechanisms that deregulate two streams, both toxic chemical regulations and

GMO gene editing pathways. But the bill reduces public notification, it removes the methodology requirements for temporary approvals, it creates new undefined concepts, and expands reliance on overseas decisions, and concentrates more authority within the EPA.

But we're seeing scientific scrutiny decrease. So there's an accountability problem. Two overseas jurisdictions cannot assure good decision-making. For example, FAO/WHO decisions are conventionally less rigorous than European Food Safety Authority decisions, which also require public feedback.

Then there is the issue of recent decisions versus older decisions. Transparency. Public oversight is substantially eroded. The bill concentrates authority and expands executive discretion at the same time that it contracts scientific oversight. Then there's an evidentiary standards problem.

So the bill enables the EPA to denew a new organism without appropriate safeguards and scrutiny. Denewing is the process of prescribing an organism as not new so that it will no longer be regulated by the HASNO Act. The legal term appears to be invented. The MB officials met with NZ EPA to discuss gene technology regulation.

The EPA explained that they weren't able to regulate newer gene-edited, or deregulate newer gene-edited organisms due to the 2014 High Court case with the Sustainability Council. The denewing concept is central to the bill. Overriding that 2014 finding without risk evaluation or cost analysis is extraordinary.

So officials and Crown law experts are inventing terminology, expanding regulatory powers, and reducing transparency, but without corresponding safeguards, regulatory frameworks, review mechanisms, transparency requirements. So it seems that they might view regulatory risk assessment as a secondary issue and any obligation to protect human and environmental health as lower order importance.

New Zealand ag exports are valued at 64 billion. Select committee members know this, that New Zealand exporters position themselves as quality suppliers to secure sales in competitive markets. The 2017 Treasury document demands cost-benefit evaluation is undertaken, but the RIS did not evaluate the premium afforded by non-GMO status and the broader trust status afforded by non-GMO messaging.

Consumer preference applies no matter where on the GMO spectrum a modified or an edited organism sits. So whether you're just making insertions or deletions inside the gene or you're adding foreign DNA, it's still a GMO. In Ag Researchers Select Committee presentation to the Gene Tech Bill, they acknowledged that their GM grass, the GM livestock feed, could not be contained. So one of the first releases could potentially undermine the capacity for meat exports to be declared GMO-free.

And MPs, you are right to be concerned about resilience and productivity, but resilience is not a single gene trait. It emerges from complex interactions between genetics, soils, nutritions, weather, ecosystems, pest management. At the same time, these challenges are intensifying. MB has reduced funding pathways for much of the public good science that needs to address them.

So deregulation is not a substitute for scientific capability. PSGR NZ requests that the Primary Production Committee pause proceedings and return the bill for fundamental revision following genuine consultation extending well beyond the narrowly selected groups engaged to date, and this includes comprehensive evaluation to understand the current state of New Zealand risk assessment, but also the evaluation of international regimes so that we do not fall into the trap of deferring to inferior jurisdictions.

So it's a question of sequence. No responsible regulatory system designs new approval pathways before the models those pathways will rely upon have been validated. First, establish a modern, transparent, scientifically robust risk assessment framework with best practice models.

Then decide, through robust risk evaluation and cost-benefit analysis, what approvals that framework can safely support. So on pages 19 to 30 of our submission, PSR speak directly to the clauses that are the most problematic. And so we leave you with this question, if the risk assessment framework is outdated and unfit for purpose, why are we expanding approval pathways before we know what needs to be fixed?

I thank you very much for this time. Thank you. I think Mark's got a question. Is that right? Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you. I'm very grateful for your time. Jodi, good morning. Thank you for your submission. Evidently, I think you said that you have a sociology degree. Is that correct? Yeah. So my post-graduate studies involved looking at New Zealand science funding- Yes... and looking at the challenges scientists have, which is why I understand how it is so difficult for people in the ag sector to get public good boots on the

ground funding- Sure... for basic monitoring, and so on. Sure. So, how do you reconcile, with your sociology hat on, that we have a practical issue in terms of sanctioning for approval certain methodologies here in New Zealand? And do you agree, yes or no, that we, in a practical sense, have difficulty, and therein it stifles activity, economic activity, productive activity, and as you rightly point out, we're considered clean and green in New Zealand.

Leaving the GMO stuff aside for the moment, can you reconcile and see that things are inordinately difficult as it sits now for primary industries to get stuff sanctioned for activity? Investment is stifled, correct. Research is stifled to actually understand complex systems.

So if you're looking at grass grub, if you're looking at the way we could change, improve or work or research or monitor farming for a challenge such as grass grub. We're not allocating MB control science funding, they shouldn't. That controls and takes everything over. If you look at the problem in the hort industry and the challenges in the hort industry, they are really complex.

It's to do with the demands in overseas markets. But we're also competing, for example, with Europe. So if you look at, for example, kiwi fruit in New Zealand, kiwi fruit in Europe, what are the Italian recommendations and restrictions? Do they have stricter recommendations? Do they have the government that funding more pathways to commence organic agriculture? So we talk about getting on sort of the, we've got to have the strongest pesticides.

We've got dozens of pesticides on the New Zealand market that are banned in Europe, and the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment has talked about we don't do environmental monitoring. We have no idea what the persistence is of the sprays in horticultural regions and, for example, hort actually, they have sort of tenfold the sprays of, for example, dry stock. So there's a lot of chemicals we need to start saying, "Is this the quality for the farmers?" Because we have occupational disease for farmers such as Parkinson's, in

the hort sector. So we need to really understand that it's much bigger than letting the approvals flow, particularly because we have not looked at the quality of that foreign jurisdiction. So I really recommend we need to look very strongly at the layers of how European Food Safety Authority, not just decides around chemicals, but also- Thanks, Jodi. I'm going to have to interrupt you. Way over time.

Thank you, Mark. Thank you very much for your submission. Thank you, Mark. Thank you. Right. We're going to move on. Have Veg New Zealand, the Veg Council turned up? Jay's here. Oh, they are. Excellent. So we're going to move on to Vegetable Council.

Jay. New Zealand Vegetable Council. If you'll come forward. Thank you. I was lucky, we were just seated outside, and we thought, "Oh, we'll go in." Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. We've set aside 15 minutes for your submission. So the time is yours.

Superb. Look, first of all, thank you very much for taking the time to hear us on this submission. The vegetable sector is one of our more difficult primary sectors to get our heads around because we've got so many crops involved. So we just want to acknowledge the work by the committee and also officials.

The legislation's one part, but the culture and direction within EPA and MPI is also very positive as well. So legislation's one part, but general direction of officials, we're also seeing a great change there, which is good. I'll hand over to Jay. So Jay's here to give the grower's perspective and here to outline what's important to us.

Yeah. Good morning. Thank you for the opportunity to speak. My name's Jay Clarke. I'm one of the owners and directors of Woodhaven Gardens, based in the Hauraki, so just up the way here. Short little drive in the rain in this morning. We crop on around about 1,000 hectares over 37 properties spread out over the Hauraki. We're growing 24 fresh vegetable crops and going around 10 to 15% of New Zealand's fresh cut vegetable supply.

We're very proud at Woodhaven that we use our wonderful natural resources in terms of our awa, our water, our whenua, our land, to grow healthy food that predominantly stays here in New Zealand and feeds New Zealanders. And I think it's really important that we understand the flow-on effects of this legislation. So without safe and effective means of controlling pest and disease, we literally cannot grow the food that feeds this country.

So, when we have regulatory barriers to get new chemistry in, it leads to old chemistry being overused and resistant profile building up in crops for disease and pests. And that means that we lower our yields continuously. Now- The skeptics amongst some of us might turn around and say, "Well, the reason that growers would advocate for this is because we get to make more money." The real effect of this is what it does to food supply, right? So if we grow a crop and we do not yield, we do not have food to feed people at

the end of the day. And the flow on effect of that is that prices go up. It becomes unaffordable for New Zealanders to access healthy food. We want those controls to be safe, not just from a human food safety perspective, but also from an environmental perspective.

So we obviously respect that there needs to be controls in place to ensure that when we bring new solutions in, whether those are bio solutions, whether those are synthetic or chemical-based solutions, that they are both safe and effective for human beings and for the environment.

But we need a process that actually delivers those in a timely manner and delivers new solutions to growers in order to be able to maintain or even expand yield. Now, I think one of the things that's overlooked in this process a lot is that actually by having effective control measures for pest and disease, we're actually able to lower the overall environmental effect of food production.

And I'll explain why. If we need 100 cabbages to feed the country and I only get a 50% yield, I have to grow 200 cabbages, and I have to put on the nitrate fertilizer for 200 cabbages. I have to apply the water for 200 cabbages. I have to cultivate land for 200 cabbages. And our biggest effect, environmental effect of vegetable growing is sediment loss and it's nutrient loss. Now, if I've got really effective control mechanisms for pest and disease, and it means that I only have to grow 110 cabbages to get my 100 cabbages to feed the country, I'm

actually going to dramatically lower my overall environmental effect. So that's why these policies are really important, not just from an economic perspective in terms of exports, not just in terms of a food security perspective for our country, but also mental perspective, because the more we can get effective control of pest and disease, the higher our yield is, the less crop we actually need in the ground to effectively feed everybody.

So this is a massive win-win piece of legislation if we get this right, and there is fantastic advancements in this space around new chemistry, around new bio controls that will allow us to get better yields in this country and deliver better outcomes for growers and all New Zealanders and the environment.

Excellent. Thank you. So we'll go to Steve. Yeah, brilliant. Thank you very much, and very much appreciate the work you guys do. You're still well worth the visit, Steve. Yeah, I'm very keen. I'd love to. I've been down in the Hawkes Bay with some of the veg growers down there, looking to keep their processing capability.

Yes, very important. You're not primarily concerned about access to new vegetable varieties or novel varieties of vegetables? Yes, we are. Because those varieties also come with different profiles for self-protection from disease and from pests.

One of the thing that's really interesting is when we look at the whole genetic editing space, and again, I'll take an example where in the States they have a genetically edited pawpaw that doesn't get bacterial ring spot. Brassica production in New Zealand is probably the biggest impact over winter for loss of production is bacterial ring spot.

And I sat with our friends at Plant and Food and said- Mm... "How easy would it be for you to take what they've done on that pawpaw and put it into a cabbage or a broccoli?" And they were like, "Click your fingers." I said, "How much would that cost?" A million bucks. The return It's big... is huge because- So you-.. because then you're not using all that nitrate fertilizer, you're not cultivating that land to get that crop.

So you do want, you would like to see easing in pathways for use of gene edited, genetically modified organisms, and do you see this bill as assisting with that? Look, I don't know if it goes all the way that we need it to, and nor do I want to expouse that we should go all the way. What I think we should is cautiously and optimistically investigate the possibilities of using new technology to drive better yields, to lower cost of healthy food, and lower the overall environmental impact of food production.

And then my final one is, what are your concerns about the extent of public willingness to consume products that have been gene edited or modified? Yeah, that is high. At the end of the day, we have to meet the public demand for the food that they want. Yeah. And if the public support is not there for those products, then as an industry, we need to step back and look at how we can educate them on the safety profile of that. Either remove those concerns, or we have to listen and meet the market. At the end of the day, if I grow 100 cabbages that no

one wants to eat, I'm not going to be a vegetable grower for very long. So, it is definitely a relevant factor, but education plays a role in that. If we do, through our explorations, decide that this is the best way to go forward, that it delivers the right safety and benefit profiles for the country, then education of the public would be an important role to play.

Mark. Good morning. Good morning, gentlemen. Very quickly, what tonnage per hectare would you think to see as an increase with this kind of pathway being sanctioned? And time to yield, would it be affected as well in so much as- You've got not only an increase in production, I assume, by tonnage, but also, when you're thinking of crops, there is a time to yield and harvest.

Yes. Would this be increased with this process being sanctioned? Look, either we can develop softer interventions that don't interrupt the target crop as much- Mm... we will get a quicker time to yield. If you're looking at any pest or disease control option, it will also affect the target crop that you're applying it to.

So if we have softer interventions, we will get a faster time to yield. In terms of exact measures of increase in tonnages, I cannot tell you. It will depend on the intervention. But if you take my example around bacterial ring spot, now whether we have an effective chemical control, a bio control, or we have a variety that has been genetically edited to not get it, we maybe lose 20% of our crop to bacterial ring spot at times.

That's 20% less cabbages I don't have to put in the ground, 20% land I don't have to cultivate, 20% less fertilizer I don't have to use, 20% less water I don't have to use. So the benefit to us in terms of being able to produce a more affordable crop, with a lower overall environmental impact, is huge.

Mm. And technology is moving extremely fast in all forms of crop protection, whether it's new varieties, whether it's new chemistry, or whether it's bio controls. And if New Zealand doesn't have a system to allow its growers to access those, we're going to be sitting in the dark ages, and the effect- Bit behind... the effect is overall higher cost of food for our population, and the flow on health effects that come from people going to the supermarket and having to buy brown, yellow, and white food instead of buying healthy, fresh food, because it's too

expensive. Very succinct. Thank you so much. So, fungicides have made a big difference in cereal yields, for example, over the last 15, 20 years. Are there any chemistry that's being used overseas that we aren't using here that would, A, give better protection from whether it's a weed or pest or fungus, and B, softer chemistry and would do the job not as harshly as some of the existing chemistry that's available?

The answer to that question is undeniably yes, and I won't sit here and list all of them. Mm. But at the moment, the big issue is New Zealand's a tiny market. Fresh vegetable production in New Zealand accounts for about 0.1% of New Zealand's agricultural land use. So not 1%, 0.1%.

Zero. Right. We're tiny, right? So when you put a commercial proposition to an agricultural chemical company that says they need to spend millions of dollars getting a product registered, and they simply have a tiny market, they are never going to make that money back. So products just don't get registered.

The other problem that we have as growers is the rules around minimum residue levels. So you might get a product registered for broccoli because it's a reasonable large crop, but it won't get registered, say, for cauliflower or cabbage or Asian veg or other brassica crops, which mean growers then need to ensure we've got basically no residue present, even though we have an established safety guideline on brassicas.

Which means our ability to control pests and disease right up until harvest for that crop becomes really, really difficult, and we run the risk of losing our gap license over something that-... is actually completely safe for human consumption. It's just that that product hasn't been registered over the other crops.

So, the expense and cost of product registration is a real barrier to growers having the tools that they need, getting the latest technology that's coming available worldwide because we don't develop these things really internally in New Zealand, and therefore being able to deliver the best outcomes for New Zealand as a country in terms of food supply. And I speak from the vegetable sense in terms of domestic supply, because 80 to 90% of what vegetable growers do stays here in New Zealand and feeds Kiwis.
